macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek Hamadir and Overview of Marriage Obligations

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on the new perek and debate among Rishonim about order
Two major themes weaving through the perek: chiyuv mezonot and sex/neder issues
Transition from previous perek about fathers and daughters to wife and mezonot

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: The Husband Who Vows to Forbid Benefit from His Wife

micro_segments.title: Case of husband vowing that wife cannot derive hana'ah from him and the focus on mezonot
Why the gemara ignores onah (marital relations) and focuses on mezonot
Solution: up to 30 days a third party provides; more than 30 days requires divorce and ketubah
Questions about whether the neder is temporary or indefinite
Rabbi Yehuda's opinion: one month for Yisrael, two months for kohen

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Wife Forbidden from Tasting One Fruit and Adorning Herself

micro_segments.title: Woman who cannot taste any one fruit—question of how husband makes this vow
Rabbi Yehuda's distinction: one day tolerable for Yisrael, two days for kohen
Woman forbidden from adorning herself with any one jewelry or perfume
Rabbi Yosi's opinion: indefinite vow by poor women is problematic; 30 days for rich women

macro_segments.title: Rashi and Tosafot on Why Onah Is Not Affected by the Vow

micro_segments.title: Rashi's explanation: onah is off the table because husband is already obligated and it is hers
Tosafot's approach: generic language of lehanos does not include onah
Discussion of whether mezonot is de'oraissa or derabbanan and its bearing on vow effectiveness

macro_segments.title: Forced Divorce (Yotzi v'Yitein Kesuvah): Force vs. Persuasion

micro_segments.title: Why these issues appear in Ketubot not Gittin: marriage viability not just execution
Tosafot debate: does yotzi mean physical force or persuasion?
Tosafot's strong view that force is used (al korchcha, beshoti sticks)
Rabbeinu Chananel: only persuasion and social pressure, not physical force
Tosafot's final position: physical force acceptable but with caution about get me'useh
Modern parallel: tensions in Israeli batei din about pressure and validity

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Can He Vow When He Is Obligated? The Ownership Question

micro_segments.title: Gemara question: how can he vow to forbid mezonot when he is obligated to her?
Parallel case: wife vowing to forbid her maaseh yadayim—he doesn't have to annul it
Key principle: one cannot forbid objects that belong to another; ownership is key
Since he could theoretically call off arrangement and keep mezonot, he is the boss of it

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Rav Huna's Opinion—Woman Can Opt Out; The Arrangement Question

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna says woman can say I will not be fed and will not work
If woman has the power to opt out, why can't she vow to forbid maaseh yadayim?
Answer: potential to call off ≠ actual calling off; only explicit declaration creates ownership
As long as arrangement stands you are not the boss; you only become boss when you explicitly opt out

macro_segments.title: Gemara: If She Earns Enough, He Can Opt Out—But Does He Really?

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi: her maaseh yadayim covers major expenses but not minor ones
Problem: if maaseh covers major expenses, what are the minor ones she needs?
Three-way analysis: accustomed vs. unaccustomed; obligation status shifts

macro_segments.title: Gemara: The Case of Small Comforts She Forgoes (Devarim Ktanim)

micro_segments.title: Woman accustomed to luxury in father's house but chooses to live with husband's lower standard
She is mochel (forgoes) the manicure or small luxuries as long as they are living together
When husband vows, he breaks the implicit understanding and she revokes her mochel
At moment of vow, she was mochel so it's his money; once vowed she's no longer mochel
He becomes obligated but cannot satisfy obligation; requires third party or divorce

macro_segments.title: Gemara: The Thirty-Day Rule—When Does It Become Public Embarrassment?

micro_segments.title: Until 30 days people don't know; after 30 days it becomes public knowledge and zila (embarrassing)
Once embarrassing we won't tolerate it; only before public notice can we manage it

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Alternative Explanation—Neder Taken When She Was Arusa

micro_segments.title: Alternative: he took vow when she was arusa (not yet obligated for mezonot)
Mishnah rule: once time comes to marry (year passes), he becomes obligated even if not nisuin
Contradiction: if obligation is only rabbanan at that stage, does neder take effect?
Rashi: obligation is de'oraissa only at nisuin; de'oraissa obligation blocks neder
Tosafot: mezonot is derabbanan even after nisuin; but ownership question overrides obligation level

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Third Scenario—Neder Taken Before Nisuin, Then They Married

micro_segments.title: Easiest case: vow when arusa, then they actually married
Problem: if she married anyway, hasabra v'kibla—she considered and accepted the vow
If she accepted, why would they have to divorce? What new grievance?
Gemara rejects this: unlike blemish cases, with mezonot she knew what she was getting

macro_segments.title: Gemara: How Third Party Avoids Being Shliach—Announcement vs. Command

micro_segments.title: Problem: if third party provides, isn't he acting as shliach (agent) of husband?
Rav Huna's answer: says 'whoever feeds my wife won't lose out'—no direct command
Gemara: but if announcement is to specific person, isn't it like commanding him?
Case of fire: permitted to say kol hamichabeh eino mafsid to world, not to individual
By mezonot vow case works because it's announcement to world, not specific direction

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Rava's Case of Neder and Starvation; The Difference Between Specific Command and Generic Statement

micro_segments.title: Rava: man with neder against Michael goes to grocer and says I don't know what to do for him
Grocer provides food; then demands payment from Michael
Allowed because you never specifically said someone should feed him—generic distress
But if you say kol hazan eino mafsid specifically to grocer, it resembles shlichus
Gemara: kol hazan eino mafsid to whole world is clearly permitted
Tosafot: combining specific person with specific action (kol hazan + grocer) would be problematic

topical_tags.term: Ownership of mezonot and maaseh yadayim
Yotzi v'yitein kesuvah (forced divorce and ketubah)
Kol hazan eino mafsid (formula: whoever feeds won't lose)
